data.title: European Markets Close On Firm Note As Soft Inflation Data Lifts Sentiment
data.description:
BRUSSELS/FRANKFURT/PARIS (dpa-AFX) - European stocks closed on a firm note on Wednesday, reacting to some encouraging earnings updates and economic data from the region, and on expectations of more monetary easing by a few central banks. Easing geopolitical tensions contributed as well to the positive mood in the markets.

Soft inflation data from both France and the UK helped.

Investors also reacted to reports of a leadership change at the European Central Bank. A Financial Times report said Christine Lagarde plans to leave the European Central Bank before her eight-year term as president expires in October 2027.

Defense stocks gained, supported by an agreement between India and France to strengthen defense and aerospace ties. Shares from mining and banking sectors were the other prominent gainers today.

The pan European Stoxx 600 closed up by 1.19%. The U.K.'s FTSE 100 climbed 1.23%, Germany's DAX gained 1.12% and France's CAC 40 ended 0.81% up, while Switzerland's SMI settled with a gain of 0.39%.

Among other markets in Europe, Austria, Belgium, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, Greece, Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Russia, Spain and Sweden closed with sharp to moderate gains.

Ireland and Türkiye moved up marginally, while Iceland ended flat.

In the UK market, miners turned in a pretty strong performance. Antofagasta soared nearly 11%. Fresnillo, Anglo American Plc, Glencore and Endeavour Mining gained 4.25%-4.8%, and Rio Tinto climbed nearly 4%.

BAE Systems rose 4% after reporting a better-than-expected 12% rise in full-year operating profit and raising shareholder payouts.

Bank stocks Natwest Group, Barclays, Standard Chartered, HSBC Holdings gained 2%-2.6%.

St. James's Place, Compass Group, Airtel Africa, LSEG, Shell, Scottish Mortgage, Polar Capital Technology Trust, Diploma and Entain also posted handsome gains.

BT Group, National Grid, Diageo, Severn Trent, SSE, Unilever, Legal & General and RightMove declined sharply.

In the German market, Rheinmetall climbed more than 5%. Heidelberg Materials gained about 4.3%, while Siemens, Infineon and Siemens Energy moved up 3.5%-4%.

Commerzbank, Deutsche Bank, SAP, RWE, Siemens Healthineers, Daimler Truck Holding, Porsche Automobil Holding, Merck and BMW also posted strong gains.

Bayer shed more than 7%, weighed down by the company's announcement of a proposed $10.5 billion settlement to resolve ongoing litigation over its Roundup weedkiller.

Brenntag lost about 4.8%, while Fresenius Medical Care, Zalando, Vonovia, Deutsche Telekom and BASF lost 1.7%-2.5%.

In the French market, Thales, ArcelorMittal, STMicroelectronics, Societe Generale,Schneider Electric, BNP Paribas, Dassault Systemes, Airbus, Eurofins Scientific, Safran and Stellantis gained 2%-5%.

Renault, Kering, Capgemini, TotalEnergies, LVMH, Publicis Groupe and L'Oreal also posted notable gains.

Shares of food retailer Carrefour slid more than 5% after the company reported a decline in operating profit in 2025 due to acquisition costs.

EssilorLuxottica and Pernod Ricard closed lower by about 3.5%. Air Liquide, Edenred, Orange and Saint-Gobain also ended weak.

Schneider Electric, BNP Paribas, Airbus, Safran, TotalEnergies, LVMH and L'Oreal gained 1%-3%.

Final data from the statistical office INSEE revealed France's inflation eased to the lowest in five years in January and remained well below the European Central Bank's target of 2%.

The consumer price index rose only 0.3% on a yearly basis in January, following the 0.8% increase seen in December, the data showed. This was the weakest rate since December 2020, when prices remained flat.

Similarly, EU harmonized inflation softened to 0.4%, as estimated, from 0.7% in December. The harmonized rate was also the weakest since December 2020.

At the same time, core inflation hit 0.7% in January, down from 1.1% in the previous month. This was the weakest since July 2021.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices dropped 0.3% after a 0.1% rise. Likewise, the harmonized index of consumer prices slid 0.4% following last month's 0.1% gain. Both the CPI and HICP rates matched the flash estimates published earlier.

UK consumer price inflation eased to a ten-month low in January, the Office for National Statistics reported Wednesday.

Data from the Office for National Statistics showed UK's consumer price index posted an annual increase of 3% in January, in line with expectations. This was the lowest inflation since March 2025, when it stood at 2.6%. This followed December's 3.4% increase.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices fell 0.5% in January 2026, as expected, reversing a 0.4% rise in December.

Core inflation that excludes prices of energy, food, alcohol and tobacco softened to 3.1% in January from 3.2% in December.

Another report from the ONS showed that output price inflation eased to 2.5% in January from 3.1% in December. Meanwhile, input prices dropped 0.2%, following a revised rise of 0.5% in the prior month.

Month-on-month, output prices remained flat and input prices gained 0.4% in January, data showed.

data.title: French drinks maker Pernod Ricard considers listing India business, Bloomberg News reports
data.description:
French drinks maker Pernod Ricard is considering a listing of its Indian business, Bloomberg News reported on Wednesday, citing people familiar.

Pernod is speaking with potential advisers as it weighs the merits of a separate listing for Pernod Ricard India, the Bloomberg report said, adding that deliberations are at a preliminary stage.

Reuters could not immediately verify the report. Pernod Ricard did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The company, which is the maker of brands such as Chivas Regal and Absolut Vodka, is one of India's biggest liquor players and competes with Diageo in the Indian alcohol market.

The news comes as Pernod faces antitrust cases in India and a high-profile case where authorities allege suspected violations of New Delhi's liquor regulations. The company denies any wrongdoing.

Pernod's shares have risen nearly 12% in Paris so far this year, valuing the company at around $24.4 billion. The stock lost nearly 33% of its value in 2025.

data.title: Palantir And Rackspace Team Up on Enterprise AI Rollouts
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR, Financials) is teaming up with Rackspace Technology (RXT, Financials) to help companies move artificial intelligence projects out of testing mode and into real world production.

The partnership focuses on deploying Palantir's Foundry platform and Artificial Intelligence Platform (AIP) inside enterprise environments particularly in highly regulated industries that require strong data security and sovereignty controls.

Rackspace will host Palantir's software in its Private Cloud and UK Sovereign data centers, while also leading data migration and implementation services globally. The company currently has about 30 Palantir-certified engineers and plans to scale that number to more than 250 within a year.

The goal is simple: shorten AI deployment timelines from years to months or even weeks by combining Palantir's decision-intelligence software with Rackspace's managed cloud infrastructure and compliance expertise.

The purchase boosts Palantir's commercial and production-grade adoption. Rackspace improves its corporate AI infrastructure offerings as more firms want real, scalable AI solutions rather than experimental pilots.

data.title: These Dividend Stocks and Funds Are Worth a Trip Abroad — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Ian Salisbury

America's stock market has many strengths, but dividends aren't one of them. If you're looking for high yield and income, it may pay to go abroad.

Foreign stocks are on fire, returning an average 33% over the past year, including an 8% gain so far in 2026. A weakening dollar has helped lift returns, along with a rotation out of mega-cap technology stocks. Stimulus measures in Europe, corporate governance reforms in Japan, and manufacturing strength in emerging markets are also helping.

Yields abroad are higher, too, as they keep shrinking in the U.S.

Today, the S&P 500 index's annual payout amounts to 1.2% — half the size it was a decade ago, and its lowest level in more than 25 years. Funds like the Vanguard Total International Stock Index exchange-traded fund yield more than 3%. Emerging markets offer even fatter payouts, with ETFs like iShares Emerging Markets Dividend yielding 5.4%.

Dividends aren't the only reason to go abroad. Foreign markets are cheaper than their U.S. counterparts, reflecting their relative lack of Big Tech companies and emphasis on old-economy sectors like financials, industrials, and consumer goods. That tilt has long been a drag on returns, compared with the tech-heavy S&P 500. But it may now be a tailwind, as artificial intelligence raises fears of disruption in areas from software to consulting services.

Low-cost index funds can be a good way to capture foreign dividends. The Vanguard International High Dividend Yield Index fund, for one, yields 3.5%. The iShares International Select Dividend ETF yields 4.5%.

Individual stocks are another way to go, though they pose complications. Investors may need to buy shares on a foreign exchange. That can be done through brokerages like Fidelity and Charles Schwab, but it does mean dealing with currency moves and additional taxation issues.

Still, the stocks can be worth the trouble, both for compelling dividends and diversification from the U.S. — something that may prove handy if the tech sector continues to drag down the market.

Barron's found three foreign stock funds with standout records and asked their portfolio managers for their best dividend ideas. Here's a look at the funds and some of their picks.

At First Eagle Overseas, co-manager Christian Heck and his team look for stocks trading below the company's "intrinsic value," an approach pioneered by Warren Buffett and other value investors.

The strategy has worked. Over the past 15 years, the fund has returned an annualized 7.4%, compared with 6.5% for Morningstar's foreign large blend category, putting it in the top 20% of performers. The fund boasts a yield of 5% on a trailing 12-month basis.

One of the fund's dividend gems is Swedish bank Svenska Handelsbanken, Heck says. "It's a very boring, old-school Swedish bank," he says, approvingly. "They take in local deposits and make local loans, mortgages, commercial real estate — nothing complicated."

The stock yields 5.6%. One reason for the big payout: Handelsbanken is going through a rough patch — analysts expect profits to tumble 8% this year. A mix of lower interest rates, which depress net-interest margins, and higher spending on information-technology infrastructure are weighing down earnings.

Heck says investors should look past these hiccups. Nordic central banks, such as Sweden's, have mostly completed their most dramatic interest-rate cuts. And Handelsbanken's profits are expected to snap back in 2027. Meanwhile, the dividend looks safe, consuming only 67% of earnings. "This is very sustainable, as the bank only needs to retain a small percentage of earnings, perhaps 20%, to finance growth," he says.

British American Tobacco, which yields 5.8%, is in a similar spot. Sales of the company's legacy-brand cigarettes, such as Dunhills and Lucky Strikes, are slowly shrinking. But the company has growth avenues with new products like its Velo nicotine pouches, which now represent 14% of its business. Analysts expect profits to grow 5% to 6% a year through 2028.

The dividend, which amounts to about 70% of the profits, looks safe. "It's a business that just generates tons and tons of free cash flow," says Heck.

The $930 million Putnam Focused International Equity fund has delivered stellar returns — averaging 9% a year over the past 15 years, beating 95% of its peers, according to Morningstar. Focusing on large-caps, the fund delivers a concentrated punch, owning just 35 stocks. Co-manager Spencer Morgan says the fund looks for stocks with wide business moats, good management teams, and strong balance sheets.

The fund has a healthy 6.5% yield, partly thanks to a big slug of telecom stocks. One top holding is Cogeco Communications, a Canadian cable company with a 5.6% dividend yield.

Much like Comcast in the U.S., Cogeco's revenue is under pressure as TV customers ditch cable packages. Morgan argues that investors are underestimating the resilience of Cogeco's other, higher-margin business: home internet connections. "The crown jewel is really the internet connection," he says. "They can basically charge what they want, within reason."

Compared with the U.S., home internet competition is less fierce in Canada, where mobile-phone providers haven't been as aggressive about trying to poach cable companies's subscribers with so-called fixed wireless offers, Morgan adds.

Cogeco's revenues are slowly shrinking, expected at about 2.8 billion Canadian dollars ($2.05 billion) this year and slightly less in 2027. But the company is managing to maintain earnings per share, estimated at C$8.80 this year and C$8.82 in 2027, according to consensus forecasts. That leaves plenty of cash to pay the dividend, comprising only 45% of profits. There's opportunity for growth, too. In 2023, the company spent C$190 million to acquire chunks of wireless spectrum to match its broadband footprint in Quebec and Ontario; it began rolling out mobile service in the two provinces in the second half of last year.

FDJ United is a European gaming company, offering lottery games, sports betting, and poker. It's a classic cash-cow business, controlling exclusive rights to the French lottery until 2044. Since 2024, when it acquired Kindred Group — an online gaming business — it has had a presence in the United Kingdom, Sweden, and elsewhere.

The company was hit by trouble last year, as the French government, looking to fill a budget gap, increased gaming taxes. The news sent shares down 30% and led to a revenue hit of EUR18 million for the third quarter. But analysts expect the company to return to growth in 2026.

Morgan thinks the dividend is sustainable, given the regulated-monopoly nature of the French lottery business. While FDJ United's dividend payout ratio is close to 90%, it isn't necessarily a red flag. The company is committed to paying out 75% of its operating income each year. "There really isn't much of an investment need," Morgan says. "Almost all of the earnings get paid out."

The Vanguard Global Wellington fund invests roughly one-third in foreign stocks, one-third in U.S. stocks, and one-third in bonds. The mix has served it well; launched in 2017, the fund has returned 9% a year over the past five years, which puts it in the top 15% of its Morningstar global moderate allocation category.

Nataliya Kofman, who oversees the fund's equity sleeve, says she looks for "resilient franchises" at low valuations.

One such pick: Honda Motor. While Honda is mostly known in the U.S. for its Civics and Accords, she notes that the heart of the company is selling motorcycles, particularly in fast-growing developing markets like India, where they provide an affordable alternative to cars. Profits from the motorcycle unit have doubled in the past three years, and now represent about half of Honda's total.

That growth, which she argues is underappreciated by investors, should help Honda weather some of the struggles that have plagued the auto industry, such as tariffs, rising costs, and a volatile electric-vehicle market.

"They're perceived like an auto company," she says. "You're getting the motorcycle business at a substantial, substantial discount." Honda shares yield 4.5%, with a payout ratio at less than 50% of profits. The stock is up 3% this year.

British property owner Derwent London owns more than five million square feet of office space in central London. Like New York and other major U.S. hubs, London's office market has been slow to recover from the Covid hit. Kofman argues that it has come back faster than the market recognizes. "I think what the market needs to see is some splashy transactions where properties change hands — and we are starting to see some glimmers of that."

Derwent yields 4.5%. At first glance, that looks iffy. Derwent's free cash flow turned negative in the first half of 2025, the latest period for which it has reported results. The company bled about 17 million pounds sterling ($23 million) in free cash flow after taking in GBP59 million and spending GBP76 million on capital expenditures; meanwhile, the dividend costs GBP59 million a quarter.

Kofman isn't fazed, noting Derwent's relatively low debt levels and big investments in new projects. "As that spending comes down and properties come on-line, dividend coverage goes up," she argues. Derwent would also have an option of putting development on hold to support cash flow generation and the dividend if London's office market deteriorates further, she adds.

Write to Ian Salisbury at ian.salisbury@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Shopify's AI-Fear Driven Stock Drop Seen as Good Opportunity for Long-Term Investors — Market Talk
data.description: Shopify's prospects aren't undermined by recent AI jitters that sent software stocks tumbling, according to Truist. In a report, analysts Terry Tillman and Connor Passarella say that the recent "large drawdown in software valuations related to AI fears has created attractive buying opportunity for long-term investors in Shopify." The stock is down 23% year-to-date, despite a 9% rise Wednesday. But the analysts say the company's "drivers such as international, payments, enterprise, B2B, and now agentic commerce, can sustain one of the best profitable growth profiles at scale in software and agentic AI." Truist upgrades Shopify to buy from hold, and raises the price target to $150 from $110. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Gold Surges Back Above $5,000 An Ounce As Geopolitical Concerns Resurface
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Gold prices have moved sharply higher during trading on Wednesday, largely offsetting the sharp pullback seen in the previous session.

Gold for April delivery has surged $103.60 or 2.1 percent to $5,009.50 an ounce after plunging $140.40 or 2.8 percent to $4,905.90 an ounce on Tuesday.

The rebound by the price of gold may have reflected renewed geopolitical concerns after Vice President JD Vance said Iran failed to address core U.S. demands in nuclear talks earlier this week.

'In some ways it went well, they agreed to meet afterwards,' Vance told Fox News. 'But in other ways it is very clear that the president has set some red lines that the Iranians are not yet willing to actually acknowledge and work through.'

Vance also said President Donald Trump reserves the right to use military force if diplomacy fails, noting, 'We do have a very powerful military - the president has shown a willingness to use it.'

Meanwhile, Russian and Ukrainian delegations have ended the first day of U.S.-brokered peace talks in Geneva, with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy describing the discussions as 'difficult.'

The sharp increase by the price of gold also comes as traders look ahead to the release of the minutes of the Federal Reserve's latest monetary policy meeting, which may shed light on the outlook for interest rates.

data.title: Orbex appoints FRP as administrators, advisory firm says
data.description:
Scottish rocket maker Orbex has appointed restructuring advisors FRP as administrators, charging them with exploring options for the business, including a potential sale, the advisory firm said in a statement on Wednesday, after earlier attempts to raise new funding or secure a buyer failed.

Orbex, valued at about $220 million after a 2022 fundraising, ceased trading before appointing administrators, a move that impacts the roughly 163 people it employed in the UK, the statement said.

It said earlier this month the failure to secure new investment threatened hundreds of skilled jobs in Scotland and risked derailing Britain's push to build a domestic launch sector.

data.title: Costco Sparks Sneaker Frenzy With Limited Nike Drop
data.description:
Costco Wholesale Corporation (COST, Financials) generated unexpected buzz this month with a surprise in-store sneaker release in collaboration with Nike.

The exclusive Nike SB Dunk Low x Kirkland Signature stealthily showed up in several Costco warehouses in places including New York, Oregon, California, and Washington. The shoe sold out immediately since it cost $135 and each member could only buy one pair.

The design was very much in line with Costco's brand. The sneaker is based on the Nike SB Dunk Low shape and has a gray fleece-style upper that looks like Kirkland loungewear. There is a lot of Kirkland Signature branding on the heel and insole, and the tongue label looks like a Costco price tag. Some of the images on the insoles even mention Costco's $1.50 hot dog combination.

The drop rapidly became popular on social media, appealing to Generation X purchasers who liked the classic look and Generation Z buyers who wanted to resell it. Transactions on secondary markets have gone beyond $600, which shows how much people are talking about it.

Neither firm has said how many products they make or when they will be back in stock. Experts argue that the agreement shows how culturally important Costco is becoming and how open Nike is to trying out new types of retail collaborations.

For investors, this event shows how strong Costco's brand is and how it can create events that drive visitors outside of regular sales.

data.title: Rio Tinto Takes Majority Control of Nemaska Lithium
data.description:
Rio Tinto has increased its ownership of Quebec-based Nemaska Lithium to 54%, becoming the majority shareholder. Shares of Rio Tinto ADR reacted favorably to the news, trading up 1.34% in pre-market. Rio's stake increase follows its Arcadium Lithium integration, which gave it an initial 50% interest in Nemaska.

Rio Tinto expects to invest about $300 million in its Quebec lithium operations in 2026 as it works to bring the Nemaska project closer to production. The spending will support development work at Becancour and the broader James Bay portfolio.

Management described the move as part of a broader multi-year expansion. In a December strategic update, Rio outlined plans to deploy roughly $1 billion a year over the next three years to expand lithium capacity across Canada and Argentina. If those plans proceed as outlined, total output could exceed 200,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate equivalent annually by 2028, with Becancour contributing an estimated 32,000 metric tons a year.

data.title: Sherwin-Williams Raises Dividend 1.3%
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Sherwin-Williams raised its quarterly dividend by 1.3% to 80 cents a share.

The Cleveland-based paint company last raised its dividend to 79 cents from 71.5 cents in February 2025.

The new payout, equal to $3.20 a year, represents an annual yield of 0.9% based on Tuesday's closing price of $367.94.

Sherwin-Williams plans to pay the dividend on March 13 to shareholders of record as of March 2.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Palo Alto Networks' stock falls after earnings. These analysts see a good chance to buy.
data.description:
By Hannah Pedone

Some analysts encourage investors to look past a muddy outlook and focus on the company's recent momentum and AI opportunity

Palo Alto Networks shares were down 6% on Wednesday.

Palo Alto Networks' recent acquisitions have complicated the company's financial outlook - but some analysts say the stock's postearnings slide presents an opportunity for investors to jump in.

Shares of Palo Alto Networks (PANW) were down nearly 6% in afternoon trading Wednesday.

On the surface, there are some factors that can explain the downward move. Organic revenue from subscriptions and support, as well as annual recurring revenue, were "a bit lighter than expected," J.P. Morgan analyst Brian Essex wrote following the company's Tuesday afternoon earnings report.

Read more: Palo Alto Networks' stock slides as underwhelming outlook overshadows AI messaging

And management's guidance included the impact of the company's recent acquisitions, which he said "required some explanation."

On Tuesday, Palo Alto Networks announced that it would acquire Koi, an Israeli cybersecurity firm. The company also closed its acquisition of the security provider CyberArk this month. And in January, it sealed its acquisition of Chronosphere, a cybersecurity company focused on observability tools.

Joseph Gallo, an analyst at Jefferies, said in a note that while the deals "muddied" the company's guidance for the current quarter, the targets are still "achievable."

However, he said it was "disappointing" that the Palo Alto Networks' fiscal second-quarter services revenue decelerated and missed expectations.

Gallo recommends investors show "patience" around the timeline of "material" artificial-intelligence revenue benefits for the company, which could come after hyperscalers realize gains from their spending.

J.P. Morgan's Essex added that there's now "a buying opportunity" for investors, as Palo Alto Networks is showing momentum with its approach to sell customers a "platform" of products. The company is also set up well for the second half of its fiscal year, he noted.

Bernstein analyst Peter Weed said in a note that there are expanding opportunities for Palo Alto Networks to benefit from AI, especially given the growing security demands from companies looking to avoid "malicious content" when training large language models.

Weed said that management "showed off the potential tailwinds from the shiny new acquisitions." Yet he pointed out that CyberArk and Palo Alto Networks will continue to operate as distinct businesses in the near term, as "large structural changes" will not arrive this fiscal year.

Guggenheim analyst John DiFucci took a tougher tack, saying in a note that there were a lot of "moving parts" in the company's guidance, given all the deals.

He added that an earnings report coming roughly in line with estimates was likely to raise questions for investors. "Judging from the after-hours stock move down, it already has in our view," he wrote early Wednesday.

See also: Here are some attractive stocks that can withstand AI disruption, according to J.P. Morgan

-Hannah Pedone

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Hycroft Mining Stock Is Rallying Today – What Is Driving Trader Sentiment?
data.description:
Key points:
  • HYMC said it now holds 16.4 million gold ounces and 562.6 million silver ounces in the measured and indicated categories.
  • It reported increases in inferred mineral resources of 50% for gold and 38% for silver.
  • The company said it is continuing to evaluate a roasting process alternative, which could potentially provide a third revenue stream.

Hycroft Mining (HYMC) shares rallied nearly 30% on Wednesday after it reported growth in gold and silver resources. 

The Nevada-based mining company said that it has filed a third-party, independent Technical Report updating its Mineral Resource estimate with an effective date of January 21, 2026. 

The updated assessment shows the company now holds 16.4 million gold ounces and 562.6 million silver ounces in the measured and indicated categories.

Growth In Gold And Silver 

Hycroft also reported increases in inferred mineral resources of 50% for gold and 38% for silver. The company established an initial high-grade silver resource of 90.2 million ounces in the measured and indicated categories. 

The Mineral Resource estimate is based on a flowsheet utilizing milling and pressure oxidation (POX) for sulfide and certain transition mineralization and heap leaching process for oxide and other transition mineralization.

"Importantly, following only approximately 14 months of drilling since the discovery of two new high-grade silver systems, a high-grade mineral resource with underground potential has been established demonstrating the continuity of both the Brimstone and Vortex high-grade systems which remain open along strike and at depth," said Diane R. Garrett, President and Chief Executive Officer. 

The company said it is continuing to evaluate a roasting process alternative, which could potentially provide a third revenue stream through the by-product production and sale of sulfuric acid needed in the lithium, copper, and fertilizer industries. 

Producing and selling sulfuric acid could essentially convert a significant neutralization cost in the POX process into a by-product revenue from the roasting process.

How Did Stocktwits Users React?

Retail sentiment around HYMC trended in ‘bullish’ territory amid ‘normal’ message volume. 

One user predicted that HYMC stock will be at a minimum of $150 price levels.

https://stocktwits.com/Puffyk/message/645246788

Another user said that they are very bullish on the stock.

https://stocktwits.com/Mazza020/message/645241401

Shares in the company have rocketed nearly 1450% over the past year.

data.title: Nestle proposes former SNB chief Thomas Jordan for board of directors
data.description:
Swiss consumer goods giant Nestle said on Wednesday it has proposed former Swiss National Bank Chairman Thomas Jordan to serve on its board of directors and that it plans to overhaul the company's governance practices.

Nestle has been roiled by managerial turmoil over the past year-and-a-half with two CEOs departing and a chairman stepping down early amid serious setbacks that have hit its share price and raised questions about its corporate governance.

Nestle said Jordan would be put forward alongside Fatima Francisco, chief executive officer of Procter & Gamble's Global Baby, Feminine and Family Care sector for election to the board of directors at its annual general meeting on April 16.

The firm said it had reviewed its practices and committee structure and would make changes to take effect at the AGM.

Those changes include introducing additional meetings in order to increase board engagement, and revising committee structures and responsibilities, Nestle said.

The chair's and corporate governance committee will be dissolved, with its finance duties assumed by a new audit and finance committee and its corporate governance brief by a nomination and corporate governance committee, it added.

data.title: HBAR Price 2026: Market Considers Macro Signals And Technical Recovery
data.description:
The price movement was keeping around the $0.10 position, a zone that has mostly served as a balance area after a long period of decline since highs in late 2025.

Market Focus Shifts To Institutional Signals

In an X post by Logan, there was renewed focus on digital asset products, which was preceded by regulatory disclosures, which were associated with institutional crypto exposure. Although the discussion did not relate directly to HBAR, it contributed to the overall disposition of the market towards infrastructure-oriented networks.

The capital rotation into compliant crypto vehicles has given greater focus to long-standing layer-one initiatives with enterprise positioning.

U.S. SEC filing related to a crypto ETF framework, and the price of HBAR is not drifting, $0.10, indicating little short-term repricing of the market. Source: X

Additionally, market participants are keeping track of the potential of institutional narratives to be turned into long-term on-chain demand. In the case of HBAR, the reaction has been controlled, and the price has not experienced speculative spikes but has been in a steady state. This action indicates caution, since the traders are waiting to obtain more obvious evidence that the macro trends will have a direct effect on the activity of the Hedera network and token flows.

Price Stands As Traffic Keeps Flat

As of the data provided by BraveNewCoin, Hedera Hashgraph trades at $0.10, and its value dropped by 0.50% in the last 24 hours. The session was measured in the range of between $0.09946 and $0.10, which implies tight-knocking. Market capitalization is valued at $4.36 billion, and 24-hour trading volume is about $83.97 million.

HBAR is trading at approximately $0.10 and with a steady market cap and volume, which is an indication of consolidation and not directional growth. Source: BraveNewCoin

Supply measures are the same, as there are still 43.00 billion HBAR in supply. Since the token remains much lower than its all-time high of $0.57 in September 2021, it is over 82% down off the highs. Although distanced so far, volume stability indicates that the selling pressure has moderated and buyers are selective and not aggressive at the existing prices.

Technical Indicators Point to Stabilization Phase

According to data provided by TradingView, HBAR is trading at approximately $0.10093, which represents a 1.14% downward movement every day. The chart suggests that the price just entered a recovery stage after the zone of $0.085–$0.090; however, the upside momentum decreased as it neared the $0.10 psychological level. This market is now being used as a short-term balance with no domination exhibited by the buyers or sellers.

The chart shows that price is stabilizing at the $0.10 psychological area, the bearish momentum is neutral on the MACD, and the RSI is increasing. Source: TradingView

Balance improvement is reflected by momentum indicators. The MACD histogram has now become slightly positive, and the MACD line is also approaching the signal line, which is an indication of diminishing bearish pressure.

Meanwhile, the RSI around the level of 51.85 has risen above its signal average, and this is an indication of growing strength after reading lower in the previous month. These indicators coincide with a stage of consolidation and not a decisive change of the trend, since the price is held down below local resistance at around $0.11-$0.12.

Altogether, the existing structure of HBAR indicates that it is stabilizing following a long decline. The market is waiting to have stronger directional indicators near the price of $0.10, whether it will be due to the greater crypto sentiment or network-specific developments that can sustain follow-through.

data.title: Jones Lang LaSalle Shares Rise After Higher 4Q Earnings, Revenue
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Jones Lang LaSalle shares were 5% higher, at $302.05, after the company reported fourth-quarter earnings and revenue that were higher than last year.

The professional services company said it had quarterly earnings of $401.7 million, or $8.34 a share, compared with earnings of $241.2 million, or $4.97 a share, in the same quarter last year. Analysts polled by FactSet were looking for earnings of $347.3 million, or $7.21 a share.

Revenue for the quarter was $7.61 billion, compared with last year's $6.81 billion and the $7.44 billion analysts were expecting.

Adjusted earnings for the quarter were $8.71 a share, compared with the $7.35 a share analysts had forecast.

The company said its real-estate management services segment's revenue was 9% higher, and capital-markets services were 19% higher.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Coinbase adds XRP, Dogecoin, Cardano and Litecoin as collateral for onchain loans via Morpho
data.description:
Crypto exchange Coinbase has expanded its onchain loans product offered via DeFi protocol Morpho, adding XRP, Dogecoin (DOGE), Cardano (ADA), and Litecoin (LTC) as eligible collateral.

The expansion allows eligible U.S. customers, excluding those in New York, to borrow up to $100,000 in USDC by pledging the four crypto assets as collateral without selling their holdings.

"No matter what you're holding, you should be able to leverage your crypto without having to sell," Jacob Frantz, product lead at Coinbase, told The Block. "Being able to borrow against more tokens means more opportunity to make your crypto work for you, and it's an exciting preview to a future where all kinds of tokenized assets can be used to build better financial services."

Coinbase first launched the onchain loan product last year, supporting bitcoin and, later, ether. Users can borrow up to $5 million in USDC against bitcoin and up to $1 million in USDC against ether. The product has already seen more than $1.9 billion in loan originations to date, according to company data. The loans are enabled by the Morpho lending protocol on Base, with Coinbase providing the user interface to access the protocol. Coinbase charges a one-time fee each time a customer borrows — even when adding to an existing loan — which is applied to the loan principal, according to its website.

Coinbase’s onchain loans have no fixed repayment schedule, provided borrowers maintain a healthy loan-to-value (LTV) ratio to avoid liquidation. Borrowers can draw up to a 75% LTV ratio, with liquidation triggered at 86%. LTV measures the size of a loan relative to the market value of the posted collateral; it rises as collateral falls in value or interest accrues, and falls when collateral appreciates or the loan is repaid.

Interest rates are variable and set by supply and demand on Morpho. Loan proceeds cannot be used to trade on Coinbase under its terms. The onchain loans are available to verified users in the U.S. (excluding New York), with broader international access planned.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2026 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

At 11:25 a.m. ET, the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 280.48 points, or 0.56%, to 49,813.67, the S&P 500 gained 58.89 points, or 0.86%, to 6,902.11, and the Nasdaq Composite was up 294.79 points, or 1.31%, to 22,873.17.

For a full report, click on

- - - -

The pan-European index closed 1.2% up at 628.69 points, with all major regional benchmarks finishing in positive territory.

For a full report, click on

For a full report, click on

The S&P/ASX 200 gained 0.5% to close at 9,007 points, reclaiming the 9,000 level for the first time since last Wednesday and extending gains to a third straight session.

he Aussie dollar eased 0.2% to $0.7074 , having bounced from a low of $0.7028 overnight.

data.title: US says oil companies must pay local taxes, fees to Venezuelan government
data.description:
The U.S. Treasury Department said on Wednesday that companies working in the Venezuelan oil business must pay local taxes, permits and fees to the government of Venezuela, while royalties and federal taxes must be paid into a fund managed by the United States.

The Treasury said the royalties on energy, fixed per-barrel levies and federal taxes must be paid into the Foreign Government Deposit Funds, managed by the Trump administration. The information is in a document called Frequently Asked Questions, which was seen by Reuters ahead of its publication on Treasury's website.

The FAQ refers to two authorizations, known as general licenses, that Treasury issued on February 10. One lifted U.S. sanctions on exports, sales, storage and transportation of Venezuelan oil. The other authorized the provision of U.S. goods, technology, software or services for the exploration, development or production of oil and gas in Venezuela.

data.title: Otter Tail Corp SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Otter Tail Corporation, a diversified company operating in the Electric, Manufacturing, and Plastics segments, has released its 2025 Form 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Operating Revenues: $1,304.1 million, a decrease of 2.0% from 2024, primarily due to decreased sales prices in the Plastics segment and decreased sales volumes in the Manufacturing segment.
  • Operating Income: $345.7 million, a decrease of 9.1% from 2024, driven by increased operating expenses and decreased revenues.
  • Net Income: $275.9 million, a decrease of 8.5% from 2024, impacted by lower earnings in the Plastics and Manufacturing segments.
  • Diluted EPS: $6.55, down from $7.17 in 2024, reflecting the decline in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The Electric segment contributed 43% to operating revenues in 2025, with the Manufacturing segment contributing 24% and the Plastics segment contributing 32%.
  • Geographical Performance: The Electric segment serves approximately 134,000 customers across a 70,000 square mile area in western Minnesota, eastern North Dakota, and northeastern South Dakota. The Plastics segment primarily serves the western half of the U.S. and western Canada.
  • Sales Units: The Electric segment reported retail kWh sales of 5,917,736 in 2025, a 4.2% increase from 2024, and wholesale kWh sales of 404,750, a 48.1% increase. The Plastics segment saw an 8% increase in sales volumes, driven by additional production capacity.
  • New Production Launches: The Plastics segment completed the first phase of an expansion project at Vinyltech in late 2024, enhancing production capacity and large diameter pipe capability. The Electric segment is developing the Solway Solar facility, expected to add 50 MW of generating capacity by 2026 or early 2027.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates that industry conditions within the PVC pipe market will normalize through 2027, with earnings and cash flow from the Plastics segment expected to moderate. The Electric segment plans to continue investments in renewable energy and battery storage to support a transition to a lower-carbon energy future.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Investments: Otter Tail Corporation is focusing on strategic investments in its Electric segment, with significant capital expenditures planned for renewable generation, storage, and transmission projects. The company is also expanding its production capacity in the Plastics segment, following the completion of expansion projects at Vinyltech and BTD Manufacturing. These initiatives are aimed at enhancing operational efficiency and meeting future demand.
  • Capital Management: The company issued $100 million of long-term debt to fund capital expenditures and repay short-term borrowings. It maintains strong liquidity with $705.5 million available, supported by credit agreements with accordion features allowing for increased borrowing limits. Otter Tail also paid $88.1 million in dividends, marking its 87th consecutive year of dividend payments, and has a robust equity position with a year-end equity ratio of 62.8%.
  • Future Outlook: Otter Tail anticipates a shift in its earnings mix back towards its long-term target of 70% Electric and 30% Manufacturing Platform as conditions in the PVC pipe market normalize by 2027. The company plans to continue its capital investment strategy, with $2.05 billion in capital expenditures projected from 2026 to 2030, focusing on renewable energy and infrastructure improvements. Additionally, Otter Tail aims to maintain its investment-grade credit ratings to ensure access to capital markets for future growth.

Challenges and Risks

  • Operational Risks: The company's strategy involves large capital investments, which are subject to risks such as regulatory changes, commodity pricing fluctuations, and construction delays. Weather impacts and climate change pose physical and transition risks, potentially affecting demand, operations, and insurance costs. The company also faces risks from cyber incidents and system failures, which could disrupt operations and lead to significant expenses.
  • Regulatory Risks: The Electric segment is heavily regulated, with risks associated with rate recovery, regulatory compliance, and environmental laws. Changes in regulations or opposition from regulatory bodies could impact the company's ability to recover costs and achieve financial goals. The company is also subject to evolving environmental regulations, which may require substantial capital expenditures or lead to early closure of facilities.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in demand and pricing for its products, particularly in the Plastics segment. Changes in PVC resin prices and supply chain disruptions could negatively impact profitability. The company also faces competition from domestic and foreign manufacturers, which could affect revenues and earnings.
  • Emerging Risks: The transition to low-carbon energy sources presents challenges, including the adoption of new technologies and compliance with greenhouse gas emissions regulations. The company is also impacted by changes in tax laws and trade policies, which could affect financial condition and operating results.
  • Management Strategies: Management is focused on strategic diversification and investment in the Electric segment to drive growth. The company plans to invest in renewable energy projects and battery storage to transition to a lower-carbon energy future. Management is also addressing supply chain risks and exploring opportunities for acquisitions to enhance growth and reduce risk.
  • Capital Market and Interest Rate Risks: The company is subject to capital market and interest rate risks, which could impact its ability to access capital and implement business plans. A decrease in credit ratings could increase borrowing costs and limit access to capital markets. The company also faces risks related to pension and postretirement benefit plans, which are affected by interest rates and investment returns.

SEC Filing: Otter Tail Corp [ OTTR ] - 10-K - Feb. 18, 2026
